CURRENT AFFAIRS.

« All the faces in the serried ranks that one studied
from the platform wore the same expression of confi-
dent enthusiasm.”” So we read in the descriptive re-
port of the West British mob who gathered in Earlsfort
Rink to hurroo for the eminent muzzler of dogs who, by
a freak of fortune, was for a time Chief Secretary in
this country. We note that the editor of Al Foz was
there; we are sure he felt more at home than he did at
the trial of the Killaloe libel action. When Mr. Long

the sayings of Percy Bernard, Director of the Great

Sick an¥ Sour Railway, and of William Ireland, the

“off-license ’ Bung, who is trying for the St. Stephen’s

Division of Dublin.

Amongst those present were:—Lord Bung (Ardi-
laun); Sir John Arnott, Bart.; A. Findlater, R. G.
Tyrrell, of the “Sonnet;’’ Charles Martin, “tame’’
“Idolator;’” J. H. North, auctioneer, and numerous
others of the nobility. Amongst those who apologised
for non-attendance was Lord Kenmare ¢ tame’’
Papist; Sir John Nutting, Bart., intellectual “souper ’’
apostle; John Jameson, D.L.—whiskey, we presume;
the Right Hon. the O’Conor Don, “ tame ’’ Papist and
Irish degenerate. Where in the world was Brother
Goulding, “ Grand Steward’’ of the Freemasons?

Mr. Percy Bernard, Director of the ill-fated Great
Sick and Sour Railway, in the course of his luminous
remarks, said:—The Gaelic League was a most in-
sidious organisation, and the fact that it is said to be
non-political was calculated to entrap the unwary. If
its members could be content to go about in kilts and
wear fantastic garments no harm could come of it.””
What a lot of trouble and anxiety the Irish Language
Revival is giving the Sourfaces! Why did not some
voice say—‘ What about the Great S. and W. Railway
Ordinary shares?’’

That highly intellectual nobleman by the name of
Lord Clonbrock said that “ Home Rule was a real, a
live danger.”’ Well, that is certainly more than could
be justly said of the world-renowned nobleman who
uttered the warning.

Mr. William Ireland, defeated Alderman, who
aspires to do honour to England’s Faithful Garrison
and the off-licensing Bungs by carrying the 8t. Ste-
phen’s Green Division, said :— He was convinced that
three-fourths of the wealth and intelligence of this city
were entirely opposed to Home Rule.”” Now, now,
Bung! It is not because wicked fate decreed that a
true West Briton like you should be called, above all
names in the world, that treason-smelling name, ‘ Ire-
land,” that you need go make matters worse by talking
of ““intelligence’” What, pray, do you know about in-
The meeting appropriately closed with
that anthem that really means, for the greater part, in
this country— Damn the Nationalists and- ‘Idola-
tors.”””  The poor old Sourfaces are in a dreadful funk.

We are informed that the new Chief Secretary has
given the word that there are to be no more prosecu-
tions on the head of the “crime’ of cart-owners
putting their names in Irish only on their vehicles.

The Chief Secretary should be prosecuted himself now

by Hogg of Strokestown.

On the second night of the new year there was a
“grand concert and variety entertainment’’ held at
Kilmallock in aid of the poor—no, on second reac
we see that it wasin aid of the Race Fund. The
in this connection must not, however, be confoun

with the Irish race either at home or abroad; it was

that class of race associated with  bookies.”” - Almost
needless to say, there was not even one song in Irish.
The opening item was a “ Plantation solo,”” whatever
that is, by * Mysterious Magpie Tvoupe (in costume).”
There are plenty of “ Planters’ in Ireland. Amnother

Mr. Sloan’s Recantation. Betrays His Friends to Pla-

What his new friends think of him in his penitential robes may be
Judged from the comment of the Northern Whig, which appears

Here is another quotation from this article:—

He (Sloan) has crossed the Rubicon, and burnt his boats. He
bas betrayed his friends without placating his enomics. What,

Alas, poor Sloan!

There is quite an interesting article on Colonel Saun-
derson in our bitterly sectarian contemporary. In the
course of it we read:—

Independence is a dangerous doctrine in Ulster. It would de-
prive the place-hunter and the betrayer of his price. And talk
big as he will, Colonel Saunderson has his price, and some of the
instalments have been paid. Poor men who interest thomselves
in politics are sneeringly referred to by Colonel Saunderson and
his place-hunting brigade as * paid agitators”; but even Colonel
Saunderson cannot escape the odium of being a hireling. His son,
Mr. Edward Saunderson, is now Local Govornment Board Inspec.
tor, at a salary of £1,000 & year. The poor man’s son can only
obtain a Eivil Service appointment by competitivo examination,
and-he rarely finds it possible to reach the plums of £1,000 a
vear, reserved for the rich man’s incompetent weaklings. Intel-
ligence has too long been at a discount in {he public services
of our country. Mr. Edward Saunderson went to Australia to
farm, but proved unsuccossful, and so the Irish tax-payer had
to find the father’s son a job at home, and compensate him for
his failure as a Colonist by putting him on the Jobbery and
Corruption List at a life annuity of £1,000,

It appears from this article that Colonel Sounderson
was returned to Parliament in 1868 as a Radical! And
we are told that at that time “ there was no more deter-

. mined opponent of Orangemen’’ than the Colonel.
The longer you live, the more you learn. By the way,
it is particularly interesting to learn that “Indepen-
dence is a dangerous doctrine in Ulster.”” When
Orangemen fall out we Irishmen hear a little bit of the
Orange truth.

We have received a copy of the syllabus of the Gaelic
Literary Society, lately established at Harold’s Cross,
under the presidency of Rev. Father Eugene, C.P.
The society proposes as its object the study of our lan-
guage, literature and music, and, though but so many

" months old, we believe it is working on capital lines

towards that praiseworthy end. We wish every success
to the new Society. Intending members can get all
necessary information by writing to the Hon. Sec.
Cail do1binn, Harold’s Cross, or by calling any even-
ing from 8 to 9.30.

Bridge Woollen Co., Ltd., writes:—*‘ Seeing a para-

been re-opened by Ryan Doyle, I know of no such name

the county. The Cailey’s Bridge Woollen Mills have

 blankets, flannels, yarns, etc., and I am pleased to say

Mr. Waldron, the representative in the last British
Parliament of St. Stephen’s Green Division, Dublin, is
being opposed by a Mr. Wm. Ireland, an ex-Alderman
of Dublin. The Dublin Orangemen have approved Mr.
Ireland’s candidature, and that ought to be enough to
damn it in the eyes of any normal Irishman. This Mr.
Ireland was on the platform at an Orange meeting held
last Saturday night. That hardy bigot the Rev. Mr.
Irwin, in the course of his address, said :—“ The present,
was a solemn crisis, not only for the Empire, but for
Ireland.”” Well, Mr. Ireland will doubtless look solemn
enough when he finds himself at the bottom of the poll.

The following sentence taken from a report of this
Mr. Ireland’s speech to the Orange lambs, throws a
light on the “intelligence’” of that gentleman:—*To
think for a moment of two Parliaments in one Empire
was absurd and ridiculous.””

‘We referred briefly last week to a concert that was
recently held in the Town Hall, Tipperary. A “highly
respectable’’ account of the proceedings appears in a
local paper from a “ Correspondent.’”” 1In this account
we read:—“ Mr. Charles Dwyer, the renowned come-
dian, next appeared, and gave some of his droll collec-

applause.”  Our information is somewhat different.
Here is a part of it:— On reading the report it would
appear as if nothing unusual happened. But the fact
is, ten items on the programme were groaned and hissed.
Dwyer, the coon singer, not being allowed to open his
mouth on the stage so universal was the hissing.””

DOMHNALL UA BUACHALLA AND “THE
SPIRIT” OF THE LAW.

T the Celbridge Petty Sessions on Monday,Domh-
nall Ua Buachalla, Maynooth, was, prosecuted
by Sergeant Finnigan for having his name on his cart
in letters not legible. The case excited much local
interest, and those in sympathy with Domh-
nall almost filled the Courthouse. The let-
‘ters used on the cart were the ordinary Roman
characters. This gentleman was previously fined a
trifling sum for having his name in Irish characters on
the same cart. He refused to pay the fine, and in con-
sequence Sergeant Finnigan obtained a- distress war-
rant, and carried off several pounds worth of the de-
fendant’s property which the District Inspector (Mr.
Grey) sold by public auction in the street of Maynooth.
The goods were bought by Domhnall Ua Buachalla’s
_sympathisers, and returned to him immediately.

Since the previous prosecution, the defendant sub-
stituted English characters for the Irish ones. Even
this did not satisfy the energetic representative of the
English law, and Monday’s prosecution was the result.
Mr. William Kirkpatrick presided, and the other magis-
trates present were—Lord Frederick Fitzgerald, Messrs.
Wm. Dease, Captain Murray, and Bertram Baﬂ';ou.
Lord Frederick, who did not wish the case to come into
Court at all, retired before it came on.

Mac Ui Buachalla was defended by Mr. John Gore,
of Cavendish Row, Dublin, who produced the name-
plate, got Sergeant Finnigan to spell the name (con-
gratulating him on his knowledge of the alphabet), and
also to admit that he knew the man to whom the name
belonged.  Still Sergeant Finnigan maintained that
though legible to him if was illegible to ninety-five per
cent. of the people of the district. Then Mr. Gore.

“THE ISLAND OF PROTESTANTS.”

N politics there is often much to be gained by con-
cealing the truth from others; there is seldom any
advantage in concealing it from yourself. Were it pos-
sible by any machination to persuade the people of Eng-
land that Mr. Sloan and Colonel Saunderson were Ca-
tholics, or that the Plantation counties had now been
entirely engulfed by the waters of the Bann, an un-
scrupulous man might join a conspiracy directed to
effecting that nefarious purpose. - But I have never
been able to see what is to be gained by deluding our-
selves into this geographical heresy.  Various people
may suggest different modes of dealing with your Ulster
anti-Irishman—your Southern and Western alien won’t
count in the long run—but what on earth is the good of
pretending to completely ignore his existence. For you
can no more talk Protestant Ulster into Irishry than
Ireland can be argued into Englishry, and in the days
of the newspaper and the telegraph it is impossible to
entirely conceal the existence of near a million persons.
“We are an island of Protestants, surrounded on
three sides by Roman Catholics, and on the fourth by
the ocean,”” was the apt description of the circum-
stances given by a deceased divine. He desired to
indicate, presumably, that the islanders had about as
much connection with the Irish on the one flank as
with the mermen and mermaids on the other. And
anyone who looks into the matter will find that his
geography was substantially correct, that roughly
speaking the Protestants of Ulster form a solid, con-
tiguous mass of human beings—are, in fact, a denowmi-
national (or undenominational) island. They form a
sort of anti-Ireland within our Ireland. And as was
well expressed by this journal last ‘week, it is not be-
cause of their religion that they do not form part of the
nation; the reason of their not being Irish is childishly
simple—namely, that they do not happen to be so.

How should we deal, then, with these poor islanders
who cling obstinately to their corroborees and cannibal
habits now that we have discovered them? Tet us
first admit honestly that they are alien immigrants.
There is no glossing over the fact. They are not Irish,
and they don’t want to be. They have never read a
line of Davis in their lives, and they would grow sad
if they thought they would be compelled to read him
after their deaths. The best way to appreciate the
position of these Ulsterians is to imagine them to be
countrytmen of their national hero, William. Now, if
a million Dutchmen or Transvaalers had their residence
in the North-east of Ireland, would we consider them
Irishmen? and if they were, moreover, the most
criminal, and by far the most immoral, inhabitants of
Ireland, would we put ourselves out of our way to
absorb them against their will? Would it not be more
reasonable to do as the Liberals talk of doing in the
Transvaal—grant them autonomy, and let them culti-
vate the civic and other virtues at their leisure?

Now, this question is not a mere matter of theory,
but rather one of great practical importance at the pre-
sent-moment. -As I showed last week, the Government
of this island of Protestants is the one real difficulty in

342
-the way of administrative Home Rule  As to the South
and West of Ireland, the Land Act has deprived all the
old lies about Protestant oppression, of the indis-
pensable modicum of truth. In Limerick, for instance,
.th_er;a has not been a crime of any sort whatsoever com-
mitted since last June! Again, no less a person than
Chamberlain has pointed out with virulent truthful-
/Dess that the English have not much to fear from Ire-
= land since, even during the Boer War, nothing was
attempted against them. Remove the Ulster difficulty,
and you have no longer any obstacle save thirty-shilling
mendacity to deal with; and under such circumstances
t!ie market-value of an anti-Irishman would probably
_rise to £2 10s.
. Well, if we once face the problem of Ulster honestly,
I believe that, as far as administrative Home Rule is
concerned, it is possible to solve it.  There will, of
i course, be those individual injustices and difficulties in
) detail that beset every measure of reform, but, on the
i whole, T think we can do substantial justice. My
policy is Home Rule within Home Rule—that is, Home
Rule for the alien wherever he exists in overwhelming
i numbers, which, for practical purposes, might be con-
i sidered to be a three-fourths majority. Suppose the
K scheme outlined in my last article be carried out, and
;(‘ the government of Ireland—or a large share of it—
q committed to a Lord Lieutenant bound like the King
i
I

e

to act on advice of an elected, and therefore responsible,
Antony MacDonnell (be he Under-Sécretary or Chief
Secretary). Now I propose that each county shall have
i the option of refusing to come under this scheme. If it
so refused it might be dealt with in either of two ways.
; Either it might have its affairs administered direct from
{{ London, a policy easy to adopt, and not unattractive to
f your Ulster West Briton, or else it might be permitted
to, so to speak, continue the old arrangement, and de-
it clare that, as far as its affairs were concerned, the Eng-
N lish Lord Lieutenant should act not on the directions of
the elected Irish Secretary, but exclusively on the ad-
vice of the English Cabinet or his English Chief Secre-
tary, if there were one. Of course, one or the other of
these policies would have to be adopted. If it were
the latter, a fair division of patronage and finance be-
tween the elected Irish Secretary and the English Chief
Secretary, looking after Ulster interests, would have to
be made.

It will be at once evident, however, that if existing
county divisions were retained, the arrangement would
2 work very harshly. A large minority might be unne-
i cessarily expatriated or impatriated’’ against its
¢ wishes. But several Northern counties are in number
I extremely large, and we might therefore provide that
i wherever there was a district containing at least forty
i thousand people, it might, upon petition (with the con-
sent, of three-fourths of the electors resident in it, have
i itself formed into a separate administrative county.
I In this way the number of persons governed against

their will in Ireland would be reduced to a minimum,
and, of course; the fact that no tariff-walls are likely to
be permitted in any case would make their administra-
tive division unobjectionable. Belfast is, no doubt, a
crux; but, to some extent, the Catholics live in quar-
ters, and if, somewhat as the Dublin Presbyterians in
Rathmines, about half of the Catholics came to live in
a quarter in which they were in a three-fourths. majo-
rity, they could form themselves into an administrative
county distinot from the county of the city of Belfast,
and thus enjoy the benefits of Irish rule. Indeed, it
might be only fair to definitely grant them one quarter
in any event. Needless to say, these proposals must;
like all constructive proposals, be subject to numercus
~ criticism in detail, but they are, on the other hand,
~ subject to infinite modification. T must therefore ask
the indulgence of my readers while inviting their assist-
ance. . ’ . CHANEL.

NOTICE that the Irish Homestead in its issue of

but it does not say whether or not these “ ample quali-

trade, or of the quality of the produce which it will be
his duty to protect. Well, many of its readers deny
the claim of the Irish Homestead to pose as ‘‘ The organ

ago.

THE IRISH YOTE IN BRITAIN.

HE Times’ report of the new Prime Minister’s

reply to the heckler at Dunfermline on the 29th

ultimo is exactly as quoted in the editorial note.to my

article of last week. Asked whether he was in favour of

granting a separate and independent Parliament to Ire-
land, Sir Henry replied :—

“No, neither the one nor the other (prolonged
cheers). Any legislative body for Ireland that I have
ever voted for was to be in subordination to the Impe-
rial Parliament (renewed cheers).”

I am asked if T do not think that leaves him (the
Prime Minister) open to go as far as Gladstone? Well,
I do not think so. The Gladstonian Bills for which Sir
Henry voted certainly proposed to set up legislative
bodies in Ireland which were to be “in subordination
to the Imperial Parliament ’’—but they were to be
separale from it. He voted for a separate (though
subordinate) Parliament for Ireland then; he is “not
in favour’’ of it now. The essence of Gladstone’s pro-
posals was that they were to give Ireland a separate
legislature—the right to make its own laws. I repeat
my question of last week—Can you have Home Rule
without, at least, a separate Parliament? Surely not.
If, then, the Prime Minister is not in favour of granting
a separate Parliament to Ireland it is quite certain thab
he is not a Home Ruler. And here I should like to say
that I do not think the second part of his statement
qualified the first. Rather it emphasised his change of
attitude. It amounted to no more than saying that he
once voted for a legislative body for Ireland (separate,
though subordinate), whereas now he does not favour it.
And, before passing from his reply, let me call atten-
tion to the “ prolonged cheers” and “ renewed cheers ’’
with which it was greeted. = That is how a Liberal
meeting hails a repudiation of Home Rule nowadays.

Irish in Britain on the strength of a condensed report,
which said that Liberals were to be supported “on con-
dition that they serve under the banner of Home Rule.’’
On reading the manifesto itself I find that only Labour
candidates looking for Irish votes are expected to be
““sound on the question of Home Rule.”” All Liberals,
except “a so-called Liberal who declares himself against
self-government for Ireland, or who has proved by his

unless at the same time he withdraws it from their -

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDOWMENT—XVIII.

ProTesTANT Law AnD Locic.

T N the two last articles T have proved that on the
showing and admission of Lord Justice Fitzgibbon,
there is not a particle of evidence to indicate that
Erasmus Smith had any Puritan intention in making
his foundation under Cromwell. I shall now prove that
on the same showing and admission, there is not a par-
ticle of evidence to indicate that Erasmus Smith had
any Episcopalian Protestant intention in continuing

that foundation under Charles II.

Episcopalian Protestants adduce the five following
Acts to prove that Erasmus Smith had Episcopalian
Protestant intentions in continuing his Endowment
under Charles IT.:—

1. He appointed Episcopalian Protestants as his
Trustees.

2. He appointed Episcopalian Protestant. School-
masters.

~ . 3. He appointed the Episcopalian Catechism, that of
Archbishop Usher for his schools.

4. He directed that the pupils of his schools should
go for University education to Trinity College, which
was then Episcopalian Protestant and admitted only
Episcopalians.

5. He ordered that the pupils of his schools, desiring
to -learn trades, should learn them from Protestant
masters.

I have shown that Erasmus Smith gave no directions
in the Indenture that his Trustees should be Puritan.
Neither has he given any directions in the Letters
Patent of 1667, or in the Charter of 1669, or in any
other document that has come down to us, that his
Trustees should be Episcopalian Protestants. Erasmus
Smith was “ compelled by the laws of the day,”” 7.c., of
his day, to do all those five Acts above mentioned.

He was compelled to appoint only Episcopalian Pro-
testants as his Trustees, for the law allowed no other
trustee; and as I have stated, five Puritan ministers
who were appointed Trustees by the Indenture, and who
refused to conform to Episcopacy, were expelled from
the office of Trustee under Charles II.

Episcopalian Protestants quote in triumph those parts
of the Letters Patent and Charter which prescribe that
the schoolmasters are to be approved by the Protestant
Archbishop or Bishop after they have signed the two
first Canons of the Protestant Church in Ireland. The
first Canon expresses belief in the 39 Articles; the
second in the Royal Supremacy. “ Here,”’ Protestants
say, “we have the clearest proof that Erasmus Smith
wished his schoolmasters to be Episcopalian Protes-
tants.”” But they omit the fact that the law of the
land dnd the law of the Protestant Church in Ireland at
the time of the Letters Patent and of the Charter, in
1667 and 1669, required that every schoolmaster and
every tutor should sign the two first Canons of the Pro-
testant Church, and should be approved or licensed by
* the Archbishop or Bishop before being admitted to

teach.

The 99th Canon of the Protestant Church in Iveland,
passed in 1634 and renewed after the Restoration, says:
“ Provided also that none be admitted or licensed to be
a schoolmaster or usher within this kingdom, unless he
first by his subscription testify his assent to the two first
* Canons,”” and also that “every Archbishop or Bishop
shall compel all such who now teach not having testified
their assent by subscription aforesaid, to desist from
teaching schools.”” The law of the land also required
that schoolmasters and private tutors should subscribe to
the two first Canons of the Established Church, and be
licensed by the Bishop before being admitted to teach.
The 5th Section of the Act of Uniformity, passed in
1665, provides:—Every schoolmaster keeping any
public or private school, and every person instructing or
" teaching any youth in any house or private family as a
tutor or schoolmaster.® . shall before the 29th
‘of September, 1667 . . at, or before his ad-

ission to be schoolmaster, subscribe the declaration
‘I, A.B., do declare that it is not lawful
pretence whatsoever to take arms against the
hat I do abhor that traitorous position of

THE LEADER.

347

taking arms by his authority against his person, or
against those that are commissioned by him; and that
T will conform to the liturgie of the Church of Ireland
as it is now by law established ; and I do declare that
there lies no obligation upon me, or any other person,
from the oath commonly called (The Solemn League and
Covenant) to endeavour any change or alteration of
government, either in Church or State, and that the
same was in itself an unlawful oath.””” This declara-
tion was a profession of belief in the liturgy and doctrine
of the Established Church, and, therefore, in the 39
articles and the Royal Supremacy.. It was, moreover, &
distinct repudiation of Puritanism. The 5th Section
commands also that this declaration should be sub-
scribed before the Archbishop or Bishop of the diocese.

The 6th Section of the Act of Uniformity enacted : —
“ That every schoolmaster, or other person instructing
or teaching youth in any private house or family as a
tutor or schoolmaster, be required to take the oath of
allegiance and supremacie, which oath is to be adminis- -
tered by the ordinarie; and if any schoolmaster or other
person instructing youth . shall
instruct or teach any youth as tutor or schoolmaster be-
fore licence obtained from his respective Archbishop or
Bishop, according to the laws and statutes of this king-
dom, and before such subscription made as aforesaid,
and before such taking of the oath of allegiance and
supremacie as aforesaid; then every such schoolmaster
and other instructing and teaching as aforesaid shall,
for the first offence, suffer three months imprisonment
without bail . . . .; and for every second and
other such offence, shall suffer three months’ imprison-
ment and also forfeit to this Majestie the
sum of five pounds, and every person shall pro-
cure a certificate under the hand and seal of the respec-
tive Archbishop or Bishop.””

This Section under this severe penalty commanded
that no schoolmaster or tutor should teach before he
had obtained the license of the Archbishop or Bishop,
“ according to the laws and statutes of this kingdom.””
Therefore, the laws and statutes of the kingdom com-
manded that no person should begin to teach before he
had made the necessary subscription and received license
of the Archbishop or Bishop. Again, by another Act
passed in 1562, every schoolmaster and private tutor
was obliged to take the oath of supremacy and thus
swear to the second Canon. And the oath was to be
administered by the Archbishop or Bishop (Ling., vol.
vi. pp. 41-2).

Another law was passed in 1581, making the school-
master or tutor in a private family who had not ob-
tained the approbation of the ordinary, liable to a
year’s imprisonment ; and making the person who em-
ployed him liable to a fine of £10 a month (Lingard,
vol. vi. p. 165).

The directions, therefore, given in the Letters Patent
and in the Charter regarding the appointment of school-
masters were enactments of the law of the land and of
the Protestant Church in Ireland, and, consequently,
supply no evidence of Erasmus Smith’s intentions.
And as all persons teaching should be Episcopalian
Protestants, those who taught trades should be Episco-
palian Protestants; and Erasmus Smith was compelled
“by the laws of the day ’’ to direct that the pupils of
his schools who desired trades should be apprenticed to
Episcopalian Protestant masters or should go without
them.

For the same reason as there was but one University
in Ireland, and that Episcopalian, he was compelled
“by the laws of the day’’ to send the pupils of his
schools to that University, or to leave them without
University education altogether.  Therefore, Acts 4
and 5 given above, supply no evidence of Episcopalian
Protestant intentions in Erasmus Smith. But Protes-
tants say that Erasmus Smith knew when he was send-
ing the pupils of his schools to Trinity College, that
they should become Episcopalian Protestants there;
and, therefore, it was his intention that they should
become Episcopalian Protestants. Let us see how this
argument will work out. Erasmus Smith knew when he
directed the pupils of his schools in the time of Crom-
well to go to Trinity College, that they should become
Puritans, or anti-Episcopalians there. Therefore, it

was his intention that they should become anti-Episco-

palians. He knew when he directed the pupils of his

schools in the time of Charles II. to go to Trinity Col-

Therefore, it was his intention that they should bg-

Act 3, given above, or the appointment of Arch-

tant Catechism only. For to appoint any other Cate-

Here Erasmus Smith tells us that he has adopted

Home RuLEISM AND ANTI-ENLISTING.

Sir—Notwithstanding “ Imaal’s ”” “I have spoken,’’
I venture to add some “further ’ few words on this
question of anti-enlisting.

He shows logically and satisfactorily that none of the
parties into which political Ireland is divided other
than the party of complete independence, would be
justified in carrying on an anti-enlisting campaign. He
further expresses the opinion that this campaign is
likely to damage the so-called Home Rule movement in
the eyes of the British elector from whom he hopes to
receive all graces and blessings. Later on he in-
sinuates that the anti-enlisting campaigners are a
miserably small body who are afraid to come out into
the open, so that ““Imaal’’ and—we may presume—
the Castle authorities may see “ who they are, what
they are, and, above all, how many they are.’’ ‘“Imaal’”
seems to hope they represent nobody.  If by that he
wishes to convey that the people of Ireland are opposed
to- this campaign, his blindness is pitiful. Surely he
must know that no matter how their leaders misrepre-
sent them, the great majority of the Irish people are
in favour of complete independence for this country.
The anti-enlisters need not come any more into the b
open than they have come if the only object to be 4
achieved is to show themselves to the public gaze. They
are aware that the people of Ireland are in sympathy
with their efforts. They may certainly rely on the
sympathy of the average United Irish Leaguer. But
the rank and file of the United Irish League are not
aware how much they differ from their elected leaders
on this vital question. I can easily picture the amaze-
ment and indignation of an earnest, active U. I. L.
worker in the country—there are none in Dublin—on
being told that the leader of the Irish race is a loyal
man, loyal to the British throne and Constitution, and
under odth to maintain the power of that Crown and
Constitution in this island at all costs. The rank and
file of the U. I. L. are therefore in a ridiculous position
As anti-enlisters they strive to injure the British Em-
pire. As Home Rulers they are loyal to the same Em-
pire. Can there be a doubt as to which feeling is the
stronger? The leaders of the constitutional movement
are loyal men, but they gain the support and money of
the masses by posing as disloyal men. *Imaal’’ states
that tle recent National Convention passed no resolu-
tion endorsing the anti-enlisting campaign. P
sumably such a resolution would not have been in a
cordance with the views of the meeting. Would i
have been in_accordance with-the views of their du
—the rank and file? Here are a fow motions W!
should like to have put before the next Co;
with the object of making clear the position of

JANUARY 13, 1906.

doyal Home Rulers. - They are perfectly consistent with
Home Ruleism.
- “(1) That we, the Home Rulers of Ireland, are loyal
to the British Crown and Constitution.
“(2). That we. are opposed to the complete indepen-
dence of Ireland.
“(3) That we are not opposed, on principle, to Irish-
men joining the British army or navy.”
I fear that if a Convention of the U. I. L. passed
such a series of resolutions the result would be bad for
 the organisation. It seems to me, therefore that if there
is to be any clearing of the air or coming into the open,
the leaders of the constitutional movement should be
the first to make their position clear on this question of
crippling the British army. By allowing themselves to
be misrepresented by “ unauthorised persons,” they' are
surely endangering their own movement in the eyes of
the British public.

SALE OF IRISH GOODS IN GREAT BRITAIN.

DEear SiR—There are a very large number of Irishmen
in this country who are willing enough to do the old
country a turn by purchasing its manufactures, but the
number of those who will go out of their way to the few
shops which stock Irish goods is a comparatively small
one,

Now in England and Scotland the avocation of tra-
velling draper is a fairly lucrative one, and I would
suggest that our Industrial Associations should send a
man over as an experiment.

A list of hundreds of Irish people from whom to build

.up a clientele would easily be supplied him, and if he
were a good man, and showing good lines, I am con-
vinced that he would soon get together a sufficient con-
nection to earn a comfortable living. In the early
stages the Association might guarantee him a minimum
income, but this period should not last long; and as
soon as the first man established himself, and became
self-supporting, the Association could set up another
one in another district, and so on.
' The tailoring trade, I think, might be made the
leading feature under such a scheme. There must be
many smart young fellows in Dublin and Cork who
would canvas orders, and take measure for clobhes, and
send same home for execution, and who could, at the
same time, distribute ties, hosiery, shirts, dress ma-
terials, etc.

The Industrial Association are in a specially good

- position to select a good man, and to see that he has
good: lines to show. Of course, this scheme would only

Smoke

THE LEADER.

349

be suitable for the larger centres, but it might be ex-
tended on a small scale in every Catholic parish.

Almost every Catholic priest could name persons in
his congregation who would be glad enough to take up
agencies for hosiery, shirtings, etc., and if the Indus-
trial Associations could promote the establishment of
such small agencies, it should prove a material help to
some of our incipient industries. B

In conclusion, there are just two points I should
like to emphasise:—(1) That the stuff should be good
value for money; (2) that the distributor should do his
work not  for love, but for profit.  Good value for
money and a fair profit to the seller should form the
basis of a permanent trade. Without these two condi-
tions it would be mere playing at shop, and would drop
off after a short time. E. R.

SERVANTS AND EMIGRATION.

Dear Sir—Your laudable offorts against emigration
are well known. But I would ask—Are there not thou-
sands of employers of servants in Ireland who could
pay a more just wage? What prospects have servants
who drudge for well-off shop-keepers and farmers for
£6 or £8 a year? No doubt it seems a free contract,
but is really like the freedom enjoyed by a man who, to
escape a tiger on land, jumps into the water to meet a
shark. When a few pounds a year only differentiates
sorvants from slaves they must emigrate Organisa-
tion into leagues is badly needed, and if necessary,
strikes on the part of the servants of both sexes. It
would be interesting if a committee of theologians were
appointed to know their conclusions regarding the rate
of wages paid to the servant class by people well pro-
vided with the world’s wealth. The Catholic Church
used her influence and authoritative voice to abolish
slavery in the past. Now, sir, a few pounds a year—
which must necessarily be spent on clothing—leaves
thousands of servants in a mere condition of slavery in
Ireland. No wonder they fleck to other lands. There
is no use saying that Irish employers cannot afford to
pay a just wage. There are scores of shop-keepers in
every town, and thousands of farmers in the country,
and great numbers iv the cities who are as well ab.e
to pay a just wage as employers in America or Australia.
The depopulation of Ireland is, I believe, God’s chas-
tisement on the sin of injustice to our fellow-beings.—I
am, sir, yours, etc.. Live axp Ler Live.
